Krutika Kuppalli
June 5, 2026
Kuppalli is an infectious diseases physician and former World Health Organization medical officer.
When I deployed to Sierra Leone during the 2014-2016 West Africa Ebola epidemic, I understood the risks. Every physician, nurse, epidemiologist, laboratorian, and aid worker who enters an Ebola outbreak does. We know that despite rigorous training and infection prevention measures, exposures can occur. We know that outbreaks unfold in difficult environments, often amid insecurity, fragile health systems, and limited resources. We know that if we become ill, our lives may depend on access to highly specialized medical care. Advertisement But there was also an understanding: If the worst happened, the United States would bring us home and provide the best care possible.
When I deployed to Sierra Leone during the 2014-2016 West Africa Ebola epidemic, I understood the risks.
Every physician, nurse, epidemiologist, laboratorian, and aid worker who enters an Ebola outbreak does. We know that despite rigorous training and infection prevention measures, exposures can occur. We know that outbreaks unfold in difficult environments, often amid insecurity, fragile health systems, and limited resources. We know that if we become ill, our lives may depend on access to highly specialized medical care.

But there was also an understanding: If the worst happened, the United States would bring us home and provide the best care possible.

articles.title: Tiny HHS office tasked with protecting research participants' safety is running on fumes
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article By Megan Molteni June 5, 2026 Megan Molteni megan.molteni@statnews.com Megan covers the intersection of science and society. How is biomedical research funded? Who benefits? And how do new technologies reflect and shape our values? You can reach Megan on Signal at mmolteni.13. Get email alerts for Megan Molteni Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts In 2021, the federal office charged with ensuring that the vast research enterprise bankrolled by the Department of Health and Human Services keeps study participants safe, received a report of a death by suicide involving a person enrolled in a study testing a treatment for depression and reduced mobility. Once the Office of Human Research Protections started looking into the death at the New York State Psychiatric Institute, it found problems that spread far beyond that one study, including failures in how the institute’s ethics board reviews proposed research. “We saw, holy smokes, this whole institution has issues,” said Lisa Buchanan, then director of the OHRP’s compliance division. The investigation became all-consuming. “It was probably 60-70% of our time,” she said. Advertisement The Food and Drug Administration got involved, as did the National Institutes of Health, which had provided grant funding to support the study. OHRP put a hold on all HHS-funded clinical research being conducted at NYSPI, and shortly after, the NIH suspended all grants for human subject research at the institute. Such severe actions are rare in the world of biomedical research. Clinical trials , ethics , HHS , NIH , research Submit a correction request Reprints Megan Molteni Science Writer Megan covers the intersection of science and society. articles.title: Drug companies, patient groups urge FDA to pause commissioner's voucher program
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Politics Drug companies, patient groups urge FDA to pause commissioner’s voucher program At town hall, experts recommend agency formalize and clarify the speedy drug review pathway Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Jose Luis Magana/AP By Lizzy Lawrence June 4, 2026 FDA Reporter Lizzy Lawrence lizzy.lawrence@statnews.com Lizzy leads STAT’s coverage of the FDA. Her stories explore the relationship between politics and science at the FDA, industry influence, and the agency’s ability to protect and promote public health. Confidential tips can be sent on Signal at lizzylaw.53. Get email alerts for Lizzy Lawrence Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts WASHINGTON — Leaders at the Food and Drug Administration on Thursday listened to criticisms and recommendations for how to move forward with a speedy drug review program put in place by former FDA commissioner Marty Makary. The listening session, held on the FDA’s White Oak Campus, featured 17 speakers representing patient groups, drug companies, and academic organizations. Some had positive feedback, particularly those whose drugs have already been approved through the program. But most asked the agency to pause the program, and then bring it back through normal regulatory procedures that require public feedback. Advertisement Makary launched the Commissioner’s National Priority Voucher program about a year ago, offering one- to two-month FDA reviews to companies that could prove their drugs “align with national priorities.” The priorities, which included addressing health crises and delivering innovative cures, were vague. Critics worried the process was vulnerable to political interference. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: After hospitals, patients get a turn to bring AI into the doctor's office
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
STAT Plus Health tech After hospitals, patients get a turn to bring AI into the doctor’s office Developers pitch their patient apps as diligent note-takers to keep track of encounters across the health system Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Adobe By Katie Palmer June 4, 2026 Health Tech Correspondent Katie Palmer katie.palmer@statnews.com Katie covers the impact of health technology on patients, clinicians, and businesses. Her stories explore the price tag of clinical AI, digital health at the FDA, and the boom in direct-to-consumer telehealth. Confidential tips can be sent on Signal at palmer.01. Get email alerts for Katie Palmer Signed up for email alerts Remove email alerts Subscribe or log in to STAT+ to unlock email alerts Patients are getting used to being recorded in the doctor’s office. More than a quarter of U.S. practices now use AI-based listening tools called ambient scribes, which capture visits in real time and draft clinical notes for clinicians to enter into patients’ medical records. But what happens when it’s the patient doing the recording? Advertisement Nearly every patient now has a recording device in their pocket, and commercial large language models have made it easier than ever for them to transcribe and interpret audio files. A growing number of consumer apps — with names like VisitRecall, Advoca Health, and AlignCare — are using AI to do just that, delivering summaries and action items for patients after the fact. One called Kin Health, with backing from the co-founder of GoodRx, announced a $9 million seed round in May. To read the rest of this story subscribe to STAT+. articles.title: Lawmakers promised cancer patients would be protected from Medicaid cuts. Now CMS says otherwise
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Opinion First Opinion Lawmakers promised cancer patients would be protected from Medicaid cuts. Now CMS says otherwise New work requirements are catastrophic Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article Chris Hondros/Getty Images By Gwen Nichols June 4, 2026 Nichols is a hematologist/oncologist, cancer researcher, and chief medical officer of Blood Cancer United. In May 2025, I joined more than 160 blood cancer advocates on Capitol Hill. At the time, Congress was considering changes to Medicaid, a state and federal program that is a lifeline for more than 75 million Americans , including older adults, children, veterans, people with disabilities, pregnant people, and adults with low incomes. Together, we advocated against policies that threatened to put health care out of reach for millions. One of those concerning policies was work reporting requirements for people covered by Medicaid. The then-proposed legislation mandated that states verify Medicaid enrollees’ monthly work status at least every six months to ensure that they are engaging each month in a minimum of 80 hours of community service, educational activities, and/or work. But undergoing cancer treatment is already a full-time job; many patients are unable to work because of their condition. Advertisement Our meetings with congressional staff and lawmakers were tense. Many lawmakers were surprised to hear our concerns, given that the legislation they were considering included an exemption for people with serious conditions like cancer. When we shared that exemptions previously failed to protect patients, many lawmakers told us we were misinformed or being lied to. Lawmakers were clear that the legislation’s protections were rock solid: No one with cancer would lose their coverage. advocacy , Cancer , CMS , HHS , Medicaid Submit a correction request Reprints Gwen Nichols Newsletter The smartest thinkers in life sciences on what's happening — and what's to come Advertisement Most Popular Alcohol study discarded by Trump officials is published in scientific journal Diabetes Association in uproar after members expelled from annual meeting over protest of NIH cuts What stripping civil service protections for thousands of federal workers will mean for HHS The seed oil panic is hurting my cardiac patients American horses are obese, too 2025 Edition: Ranking Biotech’s Top Venture Capital Firms Advertisement STAT@ASCO: Science vs. Cancer Chicago, IL May 29 ASCO 2026: The STAT Recap Virtual Jun 3 2026 Breakthrough Summit West San Francisco, CA May 19 Most Read in STAT+ Lilly shares safety, tolerability data on its next-gen obesity drug What stripping civil service protections for thousands of federal workers will mean for HHS Drug companies, patient groups urge FDA to pause commissioner’s voucher program For pancreatic cancer patients, an exciting drug can feel out of reach Trump administration to strip job protections of top NIH officials, grants staff Recommended First Opinion First Opinion June 9, 2026 Ending animal testing could set back xenotransplantation just as the field is poised for a breakthrough First Opinion First Opinion June 9, 2026 Why STAT is sticking with ‘health care’ as two words Advertisement First Opinion First Opinion June 8, 2026 American horses are obese, too Opinions+ Opinions+ June 8, 2026 $2 million gene therapy cures require a financing model First Opinion First Opinion June 6, 2026 I led the U.S. CDC response to the 2014 Ebola epidemic. By Gwen Nichols June 4, 2026 Nichols is a hematologist/oncologist, cancer researcher, and chief medical officer of Blood Cancer United.
By Gwen Nichols
Gwen Nichols
June 4, 2026
Nichols is a hematologist/oncologist, cancer researcher, and chief medical officer of Blood Cancer United.
In May 2025, I joined more than 160 blood cancer advocates on Capitol Hill. At the time, Congress was considering changes to Medicaid, a state and federal program that is a lifeline for more than 75 million Americans , including older adults, children, veterans, people with disabilities, pregnant people, and adults with low incomes. Together, we advocated against policies that threatened to put health care out of reach for millions. One of those concerning policies was work reporting requirements for people covered by Medicaid. The then-proposed legislation mandated that states verify Medicaid enrollees’ monthly work status at least every six months to ensure that they are engaging each month in a minimum of 80 hours of community service, educational activities, and/or work. But undergoing cancer treatment is already a full-time job; many patients are unable to work because of their condition. Advertisement Our meetings with congressional staff and lawmakers were tense. Many lawmakers were surprised to hear our concerns, given that the legislation they were considering included an exemption for people with serious conditions like cancer. When we shared that exemptions previously failed to protect patients, many lawmakers told us we were misinformed or being lied to. Lawmakers were clear that the legislation’s protections were rock solid: No one with cancer would lose their coverage.
In May 2025, I joined more than 160 blood cancer advocates on Capitol Hill. At the time, Congress was considering changes to Medicaid, a state and federal program that is a lifeline for more than 75 million Americans , including older adults, children, veterans, people with disabilities, pregnant people, and adults with low incomes. Together, we advocated against policies that threatened to put health care out of reach for millions.
75 million Americans
One of those concerning policies was work reporting requirements for people covered by Medicaid. The then-proposed legislation mandated that states verify Medicaid enrollees’ monthly work status at least every six months to ensure that they are engaging each month in a minimum of 80 hours of community service, educational activities, and/or work. But undergoing cancer treatment is already a full-time job; many patients are unable to work because of their condition.
Advertisement
Our meetings with congressional staff and lawmakers were tense. Many lawmakers were surprised to hear our concerns, given that the legislation they were considering included an exemption for people with serious conditions like cancer. When we shared that exemptions previously failed to protect patients, many lawmakers told us we were misinformed or being lied to. Lawmakers were clear that the legislation’s protections were rock solid: No one with cancer would lose their coverage.
Lawmakers were clear
